content.text: Silica dust is the chronic studio hazard. Silicosis is irreversible. Mix wet whenever possible. Wear a P100 respirator any time raw glaze powders are open. Wet-clean only: no sweeping, no compressed air, no dry sanding indoors.
Ventilation matters. Either a downdraft vent on the kiln or an open window with an extractor pulling air past the kiln. Never open a hot kiln below approximately 80 degrees C.

Faceting is a post-throwing technique. A round thrown cylinder is turned into a faceted form by pressing a flat wooden rib or paddle against the exterior wall at leather-hard stage. A cupped hand supports the wall from inside. Pressing the rib inward flattens a panel of the wall. Repeating this around the circumference at equal intervals creates six or eight clean flat panels separated by crisp ridges. The result sits between thrown and hand-built in character. A cone 06 bisque firing (about 1000 degrees C) and a cone 6 glaze firing (about 1220 degrees C) follow normal throwing practice.
leather-hard

stage, supported from inside by a cupped hand. Pressing the rib inward flattens a panel of the wall; repeating around the circumference at equal intervals creates six or eight clean flat panels separated by crisp ridges. The result is a form that sits between thrown and hand-built in character. A
cone
06 (approximately 1000 degrees C) bisque firing and a glaze firing to cone 6 (approximately 1220 degrees C) follow normal throwing practice.
When to use it

Faceting is applied to leather-hard thrown forms where the wall is firm enough to hold the new shape but still damp enough to deform without cracking. A cylinder is the standard starting form because its vertical walls produce clean, even panels. Bowls and vases can also be faceted but the curved walls make consistent panel width harder to control. The technique works on any cylinder sized from a mug to a tall vase. It does not work on wet clay: the panel slumps immediately. It does not work on clay past leather-hard: the clay cracks rather than deforming cleanly. Timing is the entire skill.
What you need

tools.name: Electric potter's wheel or banding wheel

materials.name: Smooth stoneware, thrown cylinder (leather-hard)
materials.quantity: any size cylinder at leather-hard stage

content.text: Step by step

Throw a cylinder with walls of at least 8 mm thickness. Thinner walls facet unevenly and may tear. Allow the piece to stiffen to leather-hard: it should feel cool and firm to the touch, hold its shape without sagging, and no longer dent easily under light thumb pressure. This typically takes 12 to 24 hours under normal studio conditions.

Plan the number of facets before starting. For six facets, mark six equally spaced points around the rim using a ruler and needle tool. For eight facets, mark eight. The marks at the rim give you lines to align each rib press. Precision in placing these marks determines how even the facets look.

Place the cylinder on a banding wheel so you can rotate it without picking it up. Slip one hand inside the cylinder, holding it flat against the interior wall at the position of the first marked facet. Your inside hand provides counter-pressure only: it does not push outward.

Press the flat face of the wooden rib firmly and evenly against the exterior wall between two adjacent marks, running from just below the rim to just above the base. Use a single steady press rather than multiple taps. The rib flattens the wall into a panel. The adjacent clay pivots outward slightly at the edges of the press, forming the ridges.

Rotate the banding wheel to the next interval and repeat. Continue until all facets are pressed. Stand back and check that the facets are equal in width and that the ridges between them are similar in sharpness. Minor inconsistencies can be corrected now by pressing lightly again on any facet that needs deepening.
Trim the
foot ring

after faceting, not before. Faceting shifts the base of the cylinder slightly, and trimming before faceting can produce a foot ring that no longer sits level after the walls are pressed. Centre the piece on the wheel at leather-hard and trim the foot ring in the usual way, checking that the piece stands level on a flat surface once trimmed.
Allow the faceted piece to reach
bone-dry
before bisque firing. Cover it loosely with plastic for the first 24 hours after faceting to slow any remaining drying unevenness at the ridges.
What it looks like when it works

A successfully faceted cylinder has flat panels of equal width running from rim to base, separated by crisp linear ridges that follow the vertical axis of the piece without curving. The ridges have a sharp but not razor-thin edge: they catch light clearly and cast a defined shadow. The form stands level. The interior remains round: faceting affects only the exterior wall. Glazing a faceted piece brings out the facets strongly because the flat panels receive glaze differently from the ridges.
Common mistakes

Faceting too early (clay still wet): the panel slumps back to round within minutes of pressing because the clay has no structural stiffness. The piece needs to be truly leather-hard, not just slightly firmed up.

Faceting too late (clay past leather-hard): the clay cracks along the ridges rather than deforming, because dried clay is brittle rather than plastic. If fine cracks appear during the press, stop immediately.

Uneven marks around the rim: if the facet intervals are not accurately spaced, the panels will be unequal in width. Measure the circumference with a cloth tape and divide it before marking, rather than estimating by eye.
Inside hand pushing outward: the inside hand is a brace, not a lever. If it pushes outward during the rib press, the wall bulges at the ridge rather than forming a crisp panel edge.
Trimming the foot ring before faceting: the slight base shift caused by faceting can move the foot ring off-centre. Always trim after the faceting is complete.
